items.title: Spark AI
items.content:
I joined Spark AI as a Senior Front-End Developer in July 2023.
By August 2024, I became a Team Lead.
Most recently, in November 2025, I stepped into the role of Platform Architect.

I guide a cross-functional team of eight people, which includes developers,
designers, and QA engineers. I work closely with my manager, Fahad, and I really
enjoy mentoring talented developers like Ammar on our team.

My daily focus is on big-picture planning and technical design. I have architected
major features for projects like Prompt Privacy, building custom authentication
and a unique Prompt Studio tool. On another project called Ethera, we fixed tricky
bugs and greatly improved system performance.

I always push for clean code standards, which helped us reduce our project bundle
sizes by 20%. For me, reaching 100% on-time delivery is about clear communication
and fixing problems early before they slow down the team.

items.title: Globewyze
items.content:
I worked at Globewyze from December 2022 to June 2023 as a Front-End Developer.
During this time, my main focus was on improving the SwipeFox platform. I wanted
to make the code better and easier to read, not just add new features to it.

By bringing in standard coding practices, we improved the code's maintainability
by 15%. This saved everyone on the team a lot of time in the long run.

I used Redux and Ant Design to fix complex user interaction issues, which made
the entire system function 50% better. One of my favorite tasks was building a
custom video player so users could upload their own introduction videos easily.

I tracked all our work and flagged any blockers in Jira to make sure we met our
deadlines without stress. This role taught me exactly how to make web systems
strong, scalable, and easy for other developers to manage.

items.title: Million News Media
items.content:
My time at Million News Media, from April to November 2022, gave me my first
big push into Next.js and server-side rendering (SSR). I helped build a financial
news web application called StocksTelegraph.

I used Next.js, Tailwind CSS, and React Query to build the entire front-end of
the site. By creating flexible, reusable components, I helped our team build new
quote pages 50% faster than before.

The part of this project I am most proud of is the SEO architecture. I carefully
chose key pages for SSR and used shallow routing to make the site very fast.
This work boosted our overall SEO performance by 55%.

Seeing the website climb higher in search rankings because of my technical choices
was an amazing feeling. Working in a fast-paced news environment taught me how to
write highly optimized code quickly without losing any quality.

items.title: Alpha Squad
items.content:
I started my professional journey at Alpha Squad in March 2022 as an
Associate Front-End Developer. It was a short but extremely intense month of
learning and building. I created several UI components for the Moda Admin
template using plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

At the same time, I spent my evenings teaching myself React.js and Next.js.
I wanted to understand the modern web as fast as possible. I also learned
Tailwind CSS during this time, which helped me improve our design workflows
and increase overall team efficiency by 30%.

This early experience was very special because it proved to me that I could
learn new skills fast and contribute to a real team right away. It gave me
the strong technical foundation and the confidence I needed to grow quickly
in my frontend development career.

items.title: Frontend
items.content:
My main development tools are built entirely around the modern JavaScript
ecosystem. HTML, CSS, and JavaScript are the strong base of everything I create.
Over the years, I have started using TypeScript for almost all of my projects.

I find it very hard to work without it now because it catches tiny errors early
and saves the team a lot of stress. React.js is my comfort zone. I love how it
uses components to build interfaces piece by piece.

I regularly pair it with Next.js to make applications much faster and highly
SEO-friendly. I do not just use these tools because they are popular in the
industry right now. I take the time to learn exactly how they work deeply under
the surface.

This careful approach helps me build clean, reliable code that other developers
can easily read and understand long after I write it.

items.title: UI Libraries
items.content:
I like to have good, reliable options for styling and managing data in my
applications. My top choice for styling is Tailwind CSS. I really enjoy its
utility-first approach, and I am currently exploring the new features in
Tailwind v4.

I also frequently use shadcn/ui to build beautiful and accessible interfaces
quickly without writing everything from scratch. I am very comfortable working
with Bootstrap and Material UI (MUI) when a specific project needs them.

For handling data from servers, React Query is amazing. It makes caching data
and updating screens feel effortless. When an application's state gets very
complex, I use Redux Toolkit. For API calls, I rely on Axios.

I try to be very careful when adding new libraries to a project because every
single tool is a long-term commitment for the whole development team.

items.title: Dev Tools
items.content:
Good tools make a developer's life much easier and keep the work organized.
I do all my daily coding on a MacBook Pro M1. I really appreciate its speed and
how well it handles heavy development tasks without slowing down.

For version control, Git is an absolute must-have. I care a lot about keeping a
clean Git history because it acts like clear documentation for the rest of the
team.

I use ESLint to keep our code style consistent and to catch simple mistakes before
they become big problems. To manage my tasks and team projects, I have used both
Jira and Trello. It usually depends on what the team prefers.

I use them to track my work, spot problems early, and keep everyone on the same
page. I believe that keeping the workflow simple helps the team focus entirely on
solving actual user problems.

items.title: Overview
items.content:
Hello! I am Mohtashim Ali, a frontend developer and platform architect
currently based in Pakistan. I have over four years of professional experience
turning complex ideas into clean, fast, and responsive web applications.

I first got into coding because I loved seeing an instant result—you click a
button on the screen, and something immediately happens. Today, my mission is
much bigger. I want to build digital experiences that are extremely fast, highly
accessible, and a pure joy for people to use.

I care deeply about writing clean code and creating good system architecture.
Right now, in my free time, I am building a personal finance app using Next.js,
SQLite, and Drizzle ORM to automatically track my transactions.

I am a very analytical person, and I am always looking for exciting new challenges
that push my technical skills and leadership abilities forward.

items.title: Life
items.content:
When I am not working on code, I still love to figure out exactly how complex
things work. I really enjoy playing PC and mobile games, especially high-graphics
titles like *Wuthering Waves*, *Arknights: Endfield*, and *Punishing: Gray Raven*.

My love for gaming and tech led me to research and plan a custom PC build. I spent
time comparing parts like the Ryzen 5 5600 and Intel i5-14600k processors to get
the best performance possible.

I also love hands-on technical projects around my house. Recently, I set up and
optimized a 4kW home solar power system. I learned all about managing the
electrical load and upgrading to modern lithium batteries.

Right now, I am actively researching local electric bikes for a cleaner daily
commute. Family is very important to me, and I love planning trips and spending
quality time with my wife.

These hobbies help me relax and bring fresh energy back to my work.

items.title: High School
items.content:
Before I went to university, I studied at Pak Kashmir Institute of Computer
Sciences in Jhelum, Punjab. From 2016 to 2018, I focused heavily on
Pre-Engineering. This was a very natural path for me because I always wanted to
understand how the world works at a fundamental level.

Studying subjects like physics and advanced mathematics helped me build very
strong logical thinking skills. Back then, I did not know for sure that I would
become a software engineer. However, those years taught me how to solve difficult
problems and how to study hard under pressure.

The jump from Pre-Engineering in high school to Software Engineering at university
felt very natural to me. I already had a solid foundation in logic, and I was ready
to apply those analytical skills to writing computer code and building digital
systems.

These early lessons in discipline still help me manage strict deadlines today.

items.title: University
items.content:
I earned my Bachelor of Science in Software Engineering from the University of
Engineering & Technology (UET) in 2022. UET gave me an excellent theoretical
foundation in core concepts like data structures, complex algorithms, and software
architecture.

However, the real magic happened when I practiced my skills outside of the
classroom. By the time I graduated, I had already started working professionally
and building real-world projects. The degree proved that I could understand very
complex systems on paper, and my personal projects proved that I could actually
build them.

It was a tough and demanding academic program, but it taught me deep discipline.
It showed me exactly how important engineering fundamentals are for a successful,
long-term career in tech.

Today, I still use the problem-solving strategies I learned during those four
important years at university. I am proud of the hard work I put in at UET because
it completely shaped how I approach software development now.
